chunk_summary: Guidance on referral thresholds for VFSS/FEES and interim bedside dysphagia strategies, focusing on airway protection, feeding safety, and energy efficiency in children with feeding difficulties.
learning_objectives: Identify high-priority referral triggers for VFSS/FEES in pediatric patients with feeding difficulties.
Implement safe bedside dysphagia strategies while awaiting instrumental assessment.
Understand the risks and benefits of texture and liquid modifications in children with dysphagia.
Document clinical questions effectively for VFSS/FEES in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: High-priority referral triggers include recurrent pneumonia, coughing with multiple textures, wet voice after swallowing, and growth faltering with respiratory symptoms.
Suspected silent aspiration is particularly concerning in children with CP, hypotonia, or prior NICU respiratory disease.
Bedside strategies include upright positioning, slow pacing with small boluses, and stopping feeding at stress cues like tachypnea or desaturation.
Avoid prolonged empiric thickening of liquids without specialist guidance due to risks of reduced intake and constipation.
Use standardized levels like IDDSI for texture modifications but avoid advancing textures in children showing signs of fatigue or pocketing.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Treat the following as high-priority referral triggers: recurrent pneumonia or wheeze temporally related to feeds, coughing or choking with multiple textures, wet or gurgly voice/cry after swallowing, color change or apnea with feeding, and growth faltering where respiratory symptoms worsen with oral intake. (Source: ASHA Practice Portal)
